title: 怪钟疑案
summary:
Detective Inspector Hakast interviews Miss Pema-Chhoe, a teacher at the Allenberg Institute for Disabled Children who is losing her vision and has studied Braille. Hakast explains that Colin Lamb, an ocean biologist and friend of his, was present at the scene because he happened to be passing by when Hilary Webber screamed about the murder. Hakast provides a physical description of the deceased—a man approximately sixty years old, between 5'9" and 5'10", with gray hair, brown eyes, a thin face, and a strong chin, wearing a dark gray suit—but Miss Pema-Chhoe is unable to identify him.

When Hakast asks about a potential connection to the Cavendish Typing Agency or a Miss Sheila Weber, Miss Pema-Chhoe denies knowing the name and states she does not own a phone to have made such a call. Regarding her whereabouts during the estimated time of death (1:30 p.m. to 2:45 p.m.), she claims she was at a post office on Albion Road and a fabric store called "Field and Lane," returning home just as her cuckoo clock chimed three times.

A conflict arises when Hakast points out that several clocks in the living room are fast by over an hour, including a Dresden porcelain clock, a gilded French clock, a silver travel clock, and a clock labeled "Rosemary." Miss Pema-Chhoe, who relies on touch to perceive her surroundings, insists these items do not belong to her and were not in the room when she left at 1:30 p.m. A fingerprint technician reports that the leather travel clock and silver clock have no fingerprints and notes that all the clocks in the room stopped at 4:13 p.m. The technician also observes that the fingerprints found in the room likely belong to women.
